subject: The World Around Us
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 2 periods = 80 minutes
Total: 2 periods · 1h 20min

periods.activity_title: Republic Day Celebrations and the National Flag
periods.textbook_anchor: A Special Day in School — Identify special events and activities at school on Republic Day (p. 76)
periods.visual_aids: Textbook figure of the National Flag with colour labels

time_bands.activity: Teacher sets the scene: ask students what they know about Republic Day and record two or three responses on the board.

Students open Textbook p. 76 and read the opening description of the Republic Day school celebration. Teacher guides a whole-class discussion on why 26 January 1950 is significant, linking freedom (1947) to the Constitution.

Students examine the National Flag illustration in the textbook. Teacher facilitates discussion on what each colour represents — saffron, white with Ashoka Chakra, green. Students record the meanings in their notebooks.
Students write their responses to the Textbook p. 76 task: listing special events and activities at their own school on Republic Day. Pairs share one or two responses aloud.
Teacher draws together key ideas: the flag as a living symbol of shared values. Students name one value represented by a flag colour before leaving.

periods.implied_lo: Students can explain how a national symbol encodes shared civic values and connect a commemorative event to its historical significance.
periods.section_context: Republic Day school celebration, National Flag colours (saffron, white, green), Ashoka Chakra, Constitution of India, 26 January 1950.
periods.teacher_facilitation_note: Ask: 'What rule at school reminds you of a rule the whole country follows?' This draws the link between school norms and the Constitution organically.

periods.activity_title: National Anthem Geography and State Tableau Poster
periods.textbook_anchor: A Special Day in School — Discuss and list the various items of your state tableau; make a poster or model of it (p. 78)
periods.visual_aids: Textbook image of Republic Day parade tableaux

time_bands.activity: Students open Textbook p. 77 and complete the table naming rivers and mountain ranges mentioned in the National Anthem. Teacher reviews answers quickly on the board, reinforcing India's natural geography.

Teacher shows or describes the Republic Day parade and explains what a state tableau represents. Using Textbook p. 78, students discuss in small groups what features their state's tableau might include — landmarks, products, festivals, art forms.

Each student begins a poster or sketch of their state tableau on chart paper or A3 sheet, including at least one natural feature and one cultural element. Teacher circulates, prompting students to consider what makes their state uniquely recognisable.
Two or three volunteers share their tableau design with the class and explain their choices. Teacher highlights how each state's tableau tells a story of that region's identity.

periods.implied_lo: Students can represent a region's natural and cultural identity through a visual creative format and justify their choices.
periods.section_context: National Anthem rivers and mountain ranges, Republic Day parade, state tableau design — landmarks, festivals, art forms.
periods.teacher_facilitation_note: Prompt students: 'Why do you think your state chose that symbol or art form for its tableau? Did the land or climate shape it?' This connects geography to cultural expression.

periods.activity_title: Observing Symbols on a Currency Note
periods.textbook_anchor: Finding India in Currency Notes — Take any Indian currency note and observe it carefully, then fill in the observation table (p. 80)
materials: Indian currency notes of at least two denominations (or printed facsimiles)
periods.visual_aids: Textbook figure of the currency note with labelled features

time_bands.activity: Teacher distributes currency notes or printed facsimiles. Students are asked: 'Before reading anything, what do you notice first on this note?' Two or three observations are shared aloud.

Students open Textbook p. 80 and systematically observe the note, filling the observation table: denomination, dominant colour, languages printed, monument or landmark, animals, and other symbols including the National Emblem and Swachh Bharat Abhiyan spectacles symbol.

Class discussion: What does the portrait of Gandhi ji and the spectacles symbol tell us about values that the country considers important? Teacher connects the Swachh Bharat symbol to Gandhi ji's cleanliness principles at his ashram.
Students write one sentence in their notebooks: 'One thing on the currency note that tells me something about India is _____ because _____.' Three volunteers share.

periods.implied_lo: Students can conduct a systematic observation of an everyday object and extract information about civic values and national identity embedded in its design.
periods.section_context: Indian currency note — Gandhi ji's portrait, National Emblem, Swachh Bharat Abhiyan spectacles symbol, denomination, languages, monuments, animals.
periods.teacher_facilitation_note: Ask: 'Have you seen someone use raised print or the MANI app to identify notes? Why might that feature matter?' This naturally surfaces inclusive design without lecturing.

periods.textbook_anchor: Finding India in Currency Notes — Design an emblem for your school using symbols, words, shapes, and colours (p. 82)
periods.visual_aids: Textbook figure of the National Emblem (Ashoka's lion capital)

time_bands.activity: Teacher recaps what an emblem communicates — the National Emblem conveys strength, courage, and confidence through three lions and the Ashoka Chakra. Students open Textbook p. 82 and discuss what message they want their school emblem to send.

Students independently design a school emblem on their drawing sheet, choosing at least one symbol, one colour with a stated meaning, and a brief motto or word. Teacher circulates asking: 'What does that shape or colour mean to you?'
Students pair up and explain their emblem to their partner: 'I chose this symbol because…' Partners ask one question. Each student refines one element based on the feedback.
Quick gallery: four or five emblems are held up. Class names one strength of each design. Teacher reinforces the idea that symbols carry meaning beyond words.

periods.implied_lo: Students can select and combine symbols, colours, and words to communicate a shared identity or set of values in a visual format.
periods.section_context: School emblem design — National Emblem as reference, symbols, colours, motto, message of shared identity.
periods.teacher_facilitation_note: Encourage students to think about what is unique about their school's environment or community. Ask: 'Is there a tree, animal, or local craft that could represent your school?'

periods.activity_title: National Symbols Riddles and State Symbols Inquiry
periods.textbook_anchor: Symbols that Speak — Read the riddles and guess the national symbols of India (p. 83)
materials: Reference books or teacher-prepared state symbols chart
periods.visual_aids: Textbook illustrations of tiger and peacock

time_bands.activity: Students open Textbook p. 83 and attempt the riddles independently, writing their guesses before the class discusses. Teacher reveals answers and asks: 'Why might India have chosen the tiger and peacock as national symbols?'

Students work in pairs to find out the state animal, bird, tree, and flower for their own state, using the teacher-prepared chart or reference books. They record findings in the table format from Textbook p. 83.

Class pools findings. Teacher facilitates a brief discussion: lions were once widespread but now survive only in Gir, Gujarat — what does this tell us about the importance of protecting habitats? Students suggest one reason why conservation matters.

Students choose one local tree, bird, animal, or flower near their school and sketch it quickly, writing one sentence on why it could be the school's symbol (Textbook p. 84 task). This bridges national symbols to local observation.

periods.implied_lo: Students can investigate natural symbols used to represent identity at different levels and explain how conservation of species connects to that identity.
periods.section_context: National Animal (tiger), National Bird (peacock), lion habitat at Gir Gujarat, state symbols inquiry, local natural symbol for school.
periods.teacher_facilitation_note: Ask: 'If the lion had not been protected in Gir, what might have happened to it as a national symbol?' This opens discussion on conservation without stating conclusions for students.

periods.activity_title: Language Survey — One Word, Many Voices
periods.textbook_anchor: Our Vibrant Culture — Find out languages spoken by students in the class and name them (p. 84)

time_bands.activity: Teacher opens by asking: 'How many languages does your family speak at home?' Students raise hands for each language mentioned. A tally is kept on the board, revealing the classroom's linguistic diversity.

Students open Textbook p. 84 and conduct the class language survey, listing languages spoken by classmates. They then take one chosen word (e.g. mango) and collect its name in as many languages as possible from classmates, recording in a two-column table: Language — Word.

Class pools results. Teacher highlights how the same fruit has different beautiful names — Māmpalam in Tamil — and invites students to share what they found for Telugu, Marathi, Bengali, or other languages. The digital translation app idea from the textbook is briefly discussed.
Students write one sentence: 'Our class speaks ___ languages, and this shows that India is ___.' They share with a partner before two or three read aloud.

periods.implied_lo: Students can collect and organise data from peers about linguistic diversity and draw an evidence-based conclusion about the richness of cultural variety.
periods.section_context: Classroom language survey, word for 'mango' in multiple Indian languages, linguistic diversity as cultural strength.
periods.teacher_facilitation_note: Invite students to share a song, greeting, or rhyme in their home language. Ask: 'What would be lost if everyone spoke only one language?'

periods.activity_title: Headgear Workshop — Tradition in Your Hands
periods.textbook_anchor: Diversity Everywhere — In small groups, use a dupatta or scarf to help each other make a traditional headgear (p. 87)
materials: Waste materials: newspaper, old fabric scraps, tape, stapler
periods.visual_aids: Textbook photographs of regional headgear (saafa/pagri from Rajasthan, topi from Himachal Pradesh)

time_bands.activity: Teacher shows the textbook photographs of regional headgear and asks: 'What does the way a person dresses their head tell us about where they come from?' Students share what headgear they have seen at home or community celebrations.

In groups of four, students use a dupatta or scarf to experiment with making a traditional headgear style shown in Textbook p. 87, helping one another. Groups then attempt a second headgear using waste materials. Teacher circulates asking about the region associated with each style.

Each group holds up their creation and names the region it represents. Class discusses: How does climate or geography in that region connect to the style of headgear? Teacher weaves in the forest-garden metaphor — diversity of style, like diversity in nature, adds strength.
Students write one sentence in their notebook: 'The ___ headgear comes from ___ and it tells us ___.' This consolidates the region-to-culture connection.

periods.implied_lo: Students can link features of traditional material culture to regional geography and explain how diversity of practice reflects the variety of environments across a country.
periods.section_context: Traditional headgear — saafa/pagri (Rajasthan), topi (Himachal Pradesh), waste-material headgear, forest-garden metaphor for cultural diversity.
periods.teacher_facilitation_note: Ask: 'Have you seen elders in your family or community wear a particular headgear for a wedding or festival? What does it mean to them?' This surfaces local tradition naturally.

periods.activity_title: Dance Forms Map and Percussion Instruments
periods.textbook_anchor: Diversity Everywhere — Identify and mark the states on the map of India where dance forms originated (p. 89)
materials: Outline map of India (one per student or pair)
periods.visual_aids: Textbook map of India with state boundaries; textbook images of percussion instruments

time_bands.activity: Teacher briefly names three or four classical and folk dance forms from the textbook and asks students: 'Have you seen any of these dances? Where have you seen them — television, a festival, school?' Two or three students share.

Students open Textbook p. 89 and, working under teacher guidance, identify the states of origin of the listed dance forms and mark them on their outline map of India using the textbook reference. Each state is labelled with the dance form's name.

Students examine the textbook images of percussion instruments — dhol, tabla, mridangam, dholak, thavil, idakka, khol, nagara, pakhawaj, dhama, khanjira. They write the names from Textbook p. 89 in their notebook and note which region each instrument is associated with.
Students share one observation: 'I noticed that ___.' Teacher draws out the pattern — each region has its own musical and dance tradition shaped by local culture and materials.

periods.implied_lo: Students can locate cultural practices on a country-level map and identify how different regions contribute distinct art forms to a shared national heritage.
periods.section_context: Classical and folk dance forms mapped to Indian states, percussion instruments — dhol, tabla, mridangam, thavil, idakka, khol, nagara, pakhawaj.
periods.teacher_facilitation_note: Ask: 'Which instrument on this list sounds like something made from materials found in a forest or farm? What might that tell us about where it comes from?'

periods.activity_title: Cultural Fair — Unity in Our Diversity
periods.textbook_anchor: Spirit of Togetherness — Cultural Fair project: groups research an assigned state and present traditional dress, food, festivals, languages, dance or music (p. 92)
materials: Chart paper or poster sheets (one per group)
Reference materials or teacher-prepared state information sheets
periods.visual_aids: Textbook page showing great Indians and currency note monuments

time_bands.activity: Teacher opens Textbook p. 91–92 and reviews the 'Let us reflect' tasks briefly: monuments on currency notes, the currency symbol of India, and animals on the ₹10 note. Students contribute answers from prior knowledge, linking back to Period 3.

Groups assigned in a prior period (or assigned now) work on Textbook p. 92 Cultural Fair task — each group prepares a mini-poster covering their state's traditional dress, popular food, festivals, language, and dance or music form. Teacher circulates, prompting groups to identify one feature shared with another state and one that is unique.
Each group presents their state poster to the class in two minutes. Class listens for: one unique feature and one value or tradition shared across multiple states.

Teacher closes with the 'threads in one fabric' metaphor from the textbook: India's diversity — ayurveda, yoga, space science, UPI — weaves together into a single vibrant identity. Students name one thing they are proud of that their country has given the world.

periods.implied_lo: Students can synthesise information about a region's cultural practices and present findings in a structured format, identifying both unique features and shared values across different communities.
periods.section_context: Cultural Fair — state-wise traditional dress, food, festivals, language, dance/music; monuments on currency notes; great Indians and their contributions.
periods.teacher_facilitation_note: Ask groups: 'What surprised you most about the state you researched? Did you find anything in common with a state that seemed very different from yours?' This reinforces unity in diversity through students' own discovery.

assessment_items.question_text: India adopted its Constitution on 26 January 1950. Which colour on the National Flag represents peace and truth, and also carries the Ashoka Chakra?

options.label: A
options.text: Saffron, because it stands for peace and the dharma wheel is placed at its centre.

options.label: B
options.text: White, because it stands for peace and truth, and the Ashoka Chakra is placed on it.

options.label: C
options.text: Green, because it stands for peace and the growing earth beneath the sky.

options.label: D
options.text: Blue, because the Ashoka Chakra is blue and represents the deep ocean of truth.

assessment_items.annotation: Correct answer is B. The item tests whether students engaged with the flag colour meanings discussed during the Republic Day activity. Distractor A tests a common confusion of saffron with white; distractor C tests confusion of green with white; distractor D introduces a colour not on the flag at all, catching students who recall only the Ashoka Chakra's blue colour without connecting it to the white stripe.
assessment_items.implied_lo: Students can explain how a national symbol encodes shared civic values and connect a commemorative event to its historical significance.

competency.competency_text: Describes relationships (including between humans and animals/nature) and traditions (art forms, celebrations, festivals) in the family and community

what_each_option_reveals.A: Indicates the student has confused the meaning of saffron (strength and courage) with the meaning of white, and has incorrectly placed the Ashoka Chakra on the saffron band.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Indicates the student has confused green (growth and prosperity) with white, possibly associating peaceful imagery of nature with the word 'peace'.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Indicates the student recognises the Ashoka Chakra's blue colour but has not connected it to the white stripe; the student may believe blue is a separate band on the flag.

assessment_items.task: Your state participates in the Republic Day parade in Delhi with a tableau that shows what makes your state special. Design a poster for your state's Republic Day tableau. Your poster must show: (1) one natural feature of your state — a river, mountain range, or landscape; (2) one cultural feature — a festival, art form, craft, or famous monument; (3) a short title or caption (one or two sentences) that explains why these features represent your state.
assessment_items.scaffold: Step 1 — Think of your state. Write down two natural features (rivers, mountains, forests) and two cultural features (festivals, dances, crafts, monuments) you know. Step 2 — Choose one from each list that best tells your state's story. Step 3 — Sketch or draw both features on your poster and add your title or caption at the top.
format_of_output: A written title or caption of one to two sentences explaining the choices
assessment_items.annotation: Accept any state-appropriate natural and cultural features. Because students come from different states and regions, a wide variety of answers — rivers, mountains, festivals, dance forms, monuments — are all correct provided they belong to the student's chosen state and the caption connects them to regional identity. Teacher note: regionally varied answers are acceptable and correct here.
assessment_items.implied_lo: Students can represent a region's natural and cultural identity through a visual creative format and justify their choices.

competency.competency_text: Presents observations and findings through different creative modes (drawing, diagram, poem, play, skit, oral and written expression)

OPEN_TASK.what_this_demonstrates: The student can draw on knowledge of a region's natural geography and cultural traditions, select representative examples, and communicate regional identity through a visual-textual format — the same thinking required to understand what a state tableau conveys.
OPEN_TASK.reading_the_scaffold: Step 1 helps students generate options before committing; Step 2 pushes selection and prioritisation; Step 3 requires production. Students who skip Step 1 often produce generic posters — prompt them to name specific features first.
OPEN_TASK.strong_vs_weak_markers: Strong: poster shows a clearly identifiable natural feature (e.g. a named river or mountain) AND a recognisable cultural feature (e.g. a named festival, dance, or craft); caption explicitly links both to the state's identity. Weak: poster contains only decorative drawings with no named feature, or caption is absent or says only 'my state is beautiful' without justification.
OPEN_TASK.inclusivity: Students who find drawing difficult may label their features with words and arrows instead of detailed illustrations. Students uncertain of their own state may choose any Indian state they have knowledge of. Oral explanation of the caption to the teacher is acceptable in place of written text.
OPEN_TASK.format_rationale: The C&E dominant mode and the implied_lo both require a creative representational output and a justification — a poster with a caption satisfies both within a single 40-minute session using only paper and pencil.

assessment_items.question_text: A student looks at an Indian currency note and sees a symbol that looks like a pair of spectacles. Which of the following best explains what this symbol represents on the currency note?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a symbol for the MANI app, which helps visually impaired people identify the note.

options.label: B
options.text: It is the Swachh Bharat Abhiyan symbol, inspired by Mahatma Gandhi's values of cleanliness.

options.label: C
options.text: It is a watermark image of Mahatma Gandhi's face, visible when held to the light.

options.label: D
options.text: It is the National Emblem showing three lions and the Ashoka Chakra.

assessment_items.annotation: Correct answer is B. This item tests careful observation of the currency note as practised in the classroom activity. Distractor A confuses the spectacles symbol with a different accessibility feature (MANI app) present on the same note — both relate to accessibility/values, testing precision of recall. Distractor C confuses the spectacles symbol with the watermark, another hidden feature on the same note. Distractor D confuses the spectacles symbol with the National Emblem, also present on the note.
assessment_items.implied_lo: Students can conduct a systematic observation of an everyday object and extract information about civic values and national identity embedded in its design.

competency.competency_text: Learns about basic social and behavioural norms, values, and dispositions that benefit our social and natural environments and that help our society function smoothly (using dustbins, standing in queues, conserving water, using public transportation, keeping one's environment clean, always helping others in need regardless of background)

what_each_option_reveals.A: Indicates the student recalls that the note has accessibility features for visually impaired users but has confused the MANI app (an audio identification tool) with the spectacles symbol; both appear on the note but serve different purposes.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Indicates the student has confused the spectacles symbol with the watermark portrait of Gandhi ji; the student recognises Gandhi ji's connection to both but has not distinguished between the two separate features.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Indicates the student recalls the National Emblem from the note but has not correctly identified which visible symbol represents the Swachh Bharat Abhiyan.

assessment_items.task: Design an emblem for your school. Your emblem must include: (1) at least one symbol or image that represents something important about your school or community; (2) a colour choice with a reason — explain in one sentence why you chose that colour; (3) a school motto — one short phrase (4–8 words) that captures the values of your school.
assessment_items.scaffold: Step 1 — Think: what is special about your school? Write two things (e.g. a tree in the compound, a sport your school is known for, a value like kindness or learning). Step 2 — Choose one as your main symbol and draw it in the centre of your emblem. Step 3 — Decide on a colour for the background or border. Write one sentence saying what the colour means to you. Step 4 — Write your motto below or above the symbol.
format_of_output: A drawn emblem on paper with symbol, colour, and motto
One written sentence explaining the colour choice
assessment_items.annotation: Students may choose any symbol meaningful to their school community. Accept varied symbols, colours, and mottoes. The quality criterion is that the student can articulate a connection between their chosen symbol/colour and the values or identity they wish to communicate — mirroring how the National Emblem communicates shared national values. Teacher note: regionally varied answers are acceptable and correct here.
assessment_items.implied_lo: Students can select and combine symbols, colours, and words to communicate a shared identity or set of values in a visual format.

competency.competency_text: Presents observations and findings through different creative modes (drawing, diagram, poem, play, skit, oral and written expression)

OPEN_TASK.what_this_demonstrates: The student understands that symbols are deliberate communicative choices — each element of an emblem carries meaning — connecting to the classroom study of how the National Emblem and currency note symbols encode civic values.
OPEN_TASK.reading_the_scaffold: Step 1 generates raw material so students are not blocked by a blank page. Steps 2–4 move from selection to production to articulation. Students who skip the motto often have a clear visual but have not yet connected it to shared values — ask them: 'What do you want people to feel when they see this?'
OPEN_TASK.strong_vs_weak_markers: Strong: symbol is clearly linked to a school-specific quality; colour choice has an explicit stated reason (not just 'I like blue'); motto communicates a value or aspiration, not just a name. Weak: emblem is a copy of a known logo without adaptation; colour reason is absent; motto is only the school's name.
OPEN_TASK.inclusivity: Students who find drawing difficult may describe their symbol in words and label it. Students may work in pairs to discuss symbol choices, but each produces their own emblem. Oral explanation of colour choice to the teacher is acceptable in place of written text.
OPEN_TASK.format_type: Visual emblem with written motto and colour rationale
OPEN_TASK.format_rationale: The C&E mode and the implied_lo require both symbol selection and justification of communicative intent — a drawn emblem with a written sentence captures both within a single session.

assessment_items.question_text: Lions once lived across many parts of India. Today they are found only in the Gir forests of Gujarat. A student says: 'The lion is no longer our national symbol because it is almost gone from India.' Do you agree or disagree? Give two reasons for your answer, using what you know about national symbols and wildlife conservation.
expected_elements: A clear statement of agreement or disagreement with the student's claim.

A reason that addresses national symbols — for example, that the National Emblem (lion capital of Ashoka) represents strength, courage, and confidence and remains a symbol of national identity regardless of the lion's current habitat range.

A reason that addresses conservation — for example, that because lions survive in Gir, conservation efforts matter and the symbol reminds citizens to protect wildlife, or that the tiger (National Animal) and peacock (National Bird) are living examples of nationally protected species whose symbol status encourages conservation.
assessment_items.annotation: Expect students to disagree with the claim. A response is acceptable if it correctly identifies that the lion on the National Emblem is a historical and civic symbol (not dependent on current population distribution) and connects the idea that symbol status can encourage conservation. Accept varied phrasing. Do not penalise if the student also mentions the tiger or peacock as supporting examples.
assessment_items.implied_lo: Students can investigate natural symbols used to represent identity at different levels and explain how conservation of species connects to that identity.

competency.competency_text: Performs simple inquiry related to specific questions independently or in groups

expected_elements: A clear position (agree or disagree) stated at the start of the response.
An explanation of what the lion represents in the National Emblem — strength, courage, confidence — showing that the symbol's meaning is not dependent on where lions currently live.

A connection between symbol status and conservation — recognising that designating an animal as a national symbol draws attention to its protection (as seen with the lion's survival in Gir due to conservation efforts).

assessment_items.question_text: A class of 30 students surveyed their home languages. They found that students spoke 8 different languages. One student said: 'This means India is confusing and divided.' Another student disagreed. Which of the following best supports the disagreeing student's view, based on what you have studied?

options.label: A
options.text: Having many languages means people cannot understand each other, so we should all learn only one language.

options.label: B
options.text: Each language carries its own stories, songs, and history, so many languages make India culturally richer.

options.label: C
options.text: Translation apps mean languages do not matter any more, so diversity is not important.

options.label: D
options.text: India has only a few languages, so the survey result of 8 languages must be wrong.

assessment_items.annotation: Correct answer is B. The item is grounded in the classroom language survey activity and the section's argument that linguistic diversity is a source of cultural strength. Distractor A represents a common misconception that linguistic diversity leads to division. Distractor C misuses the section's mention of digital translation apps to argue that languages are unimportant — inverting the text's message. Distractor D reflects factual confusion about India's linguistic reality (India has over a thousand languages, not a few).
assessment_items.implied_lo: Students can collect and organise data from peers about linguistic diversity and draw an evidence-based conclusion about the richness of cultural variety.

competency.competency_text: Observes and describes cultural diversity in their immediate environment (food, clothing, games, different seasons, festivals related to harvest and sowing)

what_each_option_reveals.A: Indicates the student holds the misconception that linguistic diversity causes social division rather than enriching culture; this student has not engaged with the section's framing of diversity as strength.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Indicates the student has picked up the section's mention of translation apps but has misread its purpose — the text uses apps to show how diversity can be navigated and celebrated, not to argue that language diversity is irrelevant.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Indicates a factual misconception about India's linguistic landscape; the student may not have processed the section's statement that more than a thousand languages are spoken in India.

assessment_items.question_text: During a class activity, students used scarves to make traditional headgear from different Indian states. Priya made a saafa from Rajasthan and Arjun made a topi from Himachal Pradesh. Their teacher asked: 'Why might these two states have developed such different styles of headgear?' Write two reasons that connect the headgear styles to the geography or climate of each region.
expected_elements: A reason connecting the saafa/pagri (Rajasthan) to its regional geography or climate — for example, that Rajasthan's hot, dry, and dusty desert climate makes a large wrapped cloth useful for protection from heat and sand.

A reason connecting the topi (Himachal Pradesh) to its regional geography or climate — for example, that Himachal Pradesh's cooler, mountainous terrain encourages compact, fitted headgear suited to colder conditions.
The response should demonstrate that differences in headgear reflect differences in environment, not just personal preference or random tradition.
assessment_items.annotation: Accept any geographically grounded reasoning that connects the headgear style to the region's known climate or landscape. Do not require technical vocabulary; plain descriptive language is acceptable. The key criterion is that the student draws a causal link between environment and cultural practice, not merely names the headgear.
assessment_items.implied_lo: Students can link features of traditional material culture to regional geography and explain how diversity of practice reflects the variety of environments across a country.

competency.competency_text: Describes the relationship between the natural environment and cultural practices in their immediate environment (nature of work, food, festivals, traditions)

expected_elements: A stated connection between the saafa/pagri style and Rajasthan's hot, arid desert geography — e.g. large wrapped cloth protects from sun, heat, and dust.
A stated connection between the Himachal Pradesh topi and the state's cool, hilly, or mountainous environment — e.g. a fitted cap is suited to cooler mountain weather.
Language that indicates causation — 'because', 'so that', 'since the climate is' — rather than merely listing facts about each region separately.

assessment_items.question_text: A student marks dance forms on a map of India. She places Bharatanatyam in Tamil Nadu and Kathak in Uttar Pradesh. Her classmate says Odissi should be marked in Odisha. Which of the following best explains why mapping dance forms to their states matters for understanding India's cultural heritage?

options.label: A
options.text: It shows that only some states have culture and others do not.

options.label: B
options.text: It shows that each region of India has developed its own distinct art forms, and together they form India's shared heritage.

options.label: C
options.text: It proves that dance forms from one state cannot be performed in any other state.

options.label: D
options.text: It means that only the state where the dance originated should celebrate it.

assessment_items.annotation: Correct answer is B. This item tests the map-marking activity's core learning: that cultural practices are geographically situated and that regional variety constitutes national heritage. Distractor A represents a misconception that mapping implies cultural inequality. Distractor C confuses geographic origin with geographic exclusivity — a common misreading of cultural mapping. Distractor D represents a possessive view of cultural ownership that contradicts the chapter's unity-in-diversity theme.
assessment_items.implied_lo: Students can locate cultural practices on a country-level map and identify how different regions contribute distinct art forms to a shared national heritage.

competency.competency_text: Reads simple maps of city, state, and country to identify natural and humanmade features (well, lake, post office, school, hospital) with reference to symbols and directions

what_each_option_reveals.A: Indicates the student has misread the purpose of mapping as ranking rather than locating; this student may believe that only mapped states have notable culture.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Indicates confusion between geographic origin and performance rights; the student has not understood that mapping records origin, not exclusivity of practice.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Indicates a possessive interpretation of cultural heritage — the student has not engaged with the chapter's framing that regional art forms belong to India's shared heritage, not exclusively to their region of origin.

assessment_items.question_text: Your class held a Cultural Fair. Each group researched a different state of India and presented its traditional dress, food, festival, language, and dance or music. After all groups presented, your teacher asked: 'What is one thing that was unique to your state, and what is one value or practice that you noticed was shared across all the states we heard about today?' Write your answer using what your group researched and what you heard from other groups.
look_for: The student names a specific unique feature of their assigned state — a named festival, a specific traditional dress, a regional food, or a dance form — showing engagement with their own group's research.

The student identifies a genuinely shared value or practice observed across multiple states — for example, the importance of family celebrations, music as a part of every culture, hospitality, or harvest festivals — rather than something unique to only one state.
The response demonstrates that the student listened to other groups' presentations, not only their own; the shared value cited should logically apply to more than one state.
The student uses connecting language that moves between the unique and the shared — showing they understand that both are simultaneously true (unity in diversity).
assessment_items.annotation: Because groups are assigned different states, both the unique feature and the shared value will vary widely across student responses. Accept any state-appropriate unique feature. For the shared value, accept any value that a student can plausibly justify as observed across the states presented in class. Teacher note: regionally varied answers are acceptable and correct here. The quality criterion is the reasoning that connects unique features to shared identity — not the specific state or value named.
assessment_items.implied_lo: Students can synthesise information about a region's cultural practices and present findings in a structured format, identifying both unique features and shared values across different communities.

competency.competency_text: Describes relationships (including between humans and animals/nature) and traditions (art forms, celebrations, festivals) in the family and community

look_for: A named, specific unique feature of the student's assigned state — not a vague statement like 'our state has nice food' but a named dish, festival, dance, or garment.
A shared value or practice that the student explicitly ties to more than one state heard during the fair — demonstrating active listening beyond their own group.

Reasoning that holds the tension between uniqueness and commonality — the student should not say 'all states are the same' nor 'every state is completely different'; the best responses show both are true simultaneously.

Appropriate use of the chapter's framing vocabulary — words or ideas like 'unity', 'diversity', 'common', 'different but', 'together' — indicating the student has internalised the chapter's central idea rather than just listing facts.
